sentences.text: OK, when the-- when the Poles retreated from the town, they left a vacuum again.
And there were no Russians coming in.
The Germans were gone.
The Poles did their killing, and they left.
No, it was just a, a self-- self-guarding youth.
There, there were a couple of people who were in the Polish Army.
And they knew weapons.
And they decided that they'll keep order in the town-- that they'll keep order.
I don't know.
I was too young.
I remembered the names, and they're probably written in the book.
But it was not appointed you, you, and you.
It was-- they got together and said we have to do it.
It was a-- it was the order of the day.
They have to do it in order to protect themselves.
They knew that the Russians were marching, because the Germans told them that.
And the way it looked, the Germans were going to settle along the border right to the west of Luboml, which was the Bug River.
And that was the border that settled later on between the Germans and the Russians.
Since we were a border town, we knew that the Russians were coming either from the north or the south.
We didn't know where from.
One day we woke up, and, and the town was full of Russian soldiers.
Tanks--
We were just-- there were refugees running from-- still running from east to west.
And there would be all kinds of refugees who stayed in town.
They remained in town.
They found a place, or they knew somebody that knew somebody, and they stayed on.
It was chaos.
It was a vacuum time.
And it's really hard to say.
It was shooting and, and killing.
And then there was a vacuum.
Then there was more shooting.
And we didn't know who would be shooting next.
And then one day, we woke up, and the Russians marched in-- the Soviet-- the, the Red Army.
The town was full of them.
It was--
I think it was around the end of September or beginning of October.
They marched in, and they settled in town.
And the first day, we knew that they're settling to stay.
They start playing music, and they showed movies-- free movies.
We didn't know about such a thing-- free movies.
And then they would-- and they would keep to themselves, but they would stay, stay friendly.
They would go to stores.
They would buy whatever they could find.
They would buy everything.
Before you knew, everything was gone from the stores-- like locusts went through, through it.
And then the merchants said they wouldn't get any more merchandise.
So they shut the doors.
They tried to hold onto whatever merchandise they had.
Because we knew it was a new order in town.
And this settled.
In no time, we, we children had to go to school.
We went to the same school, but now it was called-- the main language was Yiddish.
And we had to study also German, Ukrainian, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and Russian-- and no more Polish.
And the Poles, they went to their school, and the Ukrainians to their school.
And--
Yeah, they were allowed to study in Polish.
And we had different teachers.
And the main, main language was Yiddish, except for the languages that we had to study.
In German, we'd have a woman we always doubted.
We thought she was a spy.
We called her the [NON-ENGLISH].
She had, like, like a real bunch of hair on one side.
And she would come in-- [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And the [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And they would teach us German.
And then they would teach us Ukrainian all of a sudden.
And we would have Russian.
And they would teach us Russian.
In Yiddish, they start teaching us also about the-- not Jewish history, but the history of workers and what happened in Russia and Sholem Aleichem.
And we were still children.
We were not grown-up.
It was in-- was in '39.
It was-- I was 10 years-- 10, 11.
At that point, they-- we started-- start teaching us math and some physics and other things-- these subjects I liked-- and Russian poetry, and other things.
And so, so we really had loaded up our time.
And the school time was extended.
There was no more cheder, no more going to shul.
We have to come to, to school Shabbos, Saturday.
We had to go.
That was, for us, terrible.
But they specifically made sure that we go Saturday to school.
And my father had to work on Saturday for, for the--
To school--
--not to shul.
My father had to go to work also.
They forced the Jews to work on Shabbos also.
[SIGHS] Those who were very Orthodox-- either they didn't work, so they-- it was a danger.
You had to work.
Everybody was a worker.
Whoever was not a worker-- they made lists of people who were merchants before the war, and they shipped them out to Siberia.
They were called leeches of society.
So my father was, even though it was his machine shop and works, and so on, we, we might have been considered leeches of society, also.
And from what I understand, three days after the war with the Germans broke out, the 25th of June of '41, we were supposed to have been sent to Russia because my father owned that machine shop.
And so he was considered, also, like one of the same thing, the same status.
But--
Yes, well, first, they start catching refugees who remained behind.
Refugees-- I mean, Jewish people from Poland, from former Poland.
And that was now occupied by the Germans.
They ran, and they stayed in town.
They didn't-- they ran away from the Germans.
I did not see them load them up, but they, they took out people from our-- from our house.
And one man, his name was Roisman, Avrum Roisman, he was able to hide in our house.
And they couldn't-- they, they came for him, and they couldn't catch  him.
Then we hid him in another place.
And finally, he got out to another town that he was-- he was successful in hiding out.
We had hiding places.
We always had to have a hiding-- but at that point, we, we, we knew we had to make a hiding place.
They were not the real hiding places yet.
That came later, when the Germans came in.
He was hidden, I believe-- they didn't even tell us children, so we shouldn't tell.
But I think he was hidden in a little-- a place that the-- in the kitchen, in the back, there was some hiding place.
I think that's what it was.
I could never find out because our minds were too preoccupied with so many other things.
But that's where he hid out.
And some others, also, were hidden.
And the-- but they caught a lot of people.
Hundreds of thousands of them were shipped into Russia.
That was-- even under Poland, we would have-- there was a cellar, a, a major cellar.
But this was not really a hiding-- you could really come, and you would look, and you would search a little bit, and you would find it.
The real hiding places came later, which I will tell when I get to it.
But under the Russians, we-- some people had their lot bettered.
They had food.
They, they had work.
And we were cut off from the rest of the world.
Even when we got some letters from the United States-- my mother was not supposed to say that she has a mother in America, that she has a brother and a sister in America, a brother in Argentina.
But she had one brother in Russia, also.
And he would have to help us out every once in a while and ship us a, a package of some food or some other things in order to, to help us out.
So this is the lot that we had under the Russians.
But somehow, they did not go around killing Jews, singling out people, per se.
Because you're Jewish, you have to do this and this or be shipped out.
Even those who were sent to Siberia, later on, it, showed that they were lucky because they survived.
That was one way of surviving.
Oh, yes.
We worked for the Russians.
They took over the machine shop, and we worked for them.
They nationalized it.
They didn't even bother to nationalize it.
They were there.
There was a commissar.
There was a guy who was in charge.
And my father worked for the commissar.
I don't remember.
It was a Russian name.
But that's the way it was.
And, and my father took in his sister's son, also, to, to work there, because otherwise, he's have to go elsewhere.
So in this way, he worked in the machine shop.
And this lasted for, for about two years, from '39 until 1941.
And we knew that the Russians were-- that the Germans were on the-- right near us.
At one point, my mother's brother, which she didn't see for, for 25 years, or 30 years came from Russia, from Kharkov.
And he came to, to meet with us-- meet the family.
And he kept saying, oh, you and your Russians, and all this, The Red Ones.
He says, don't complain.
Don't complain.
You don't know who you have in your back right-- the 10-- the 5 to 10-- 10 miles to the west, you have the Germans.
And in Russian, you don't say the G-- an H.
You say G.
So he said, you have 'Gitler.' He said, you don't know what it means.
It's terrible what you have, but don't complain.
But we complained no matter what, because we didn't know what was going on.
And at one time, my mother was supposed to go to Russia to celebrate the birth of his twin children.
And she was all packed.
It was the 22nd of June, 19--
My mother's brother, Shloime, Shloime Faigen.
He lived in Russia.
He was caught in Russia before World War I.
And that's how he got stuck in Russia.
He was a boy of 13.
And he got stuck.
And they closed the border.
And that's how he remained in Russia.
So my mother was expected to-- 5 o'clock, her train was supposed to leave.
But 3 o'clock, 3:00 or 4:00 in the morning, they started bombing and shooting.
And, and, and they had cannons and bombs.
And we ran outside.
It was already bright because it was summer, 22nd of June, the largest day of the year.
And everybody ran out almost naked, with, with their underwear.
And the bombs were, were flying all over.
And airplanes-- hundreds and hundreds of planes were flying to the east.
And the Germans, they were on march.
They attacked and took the whole front-- all Eastern, Eastern Poland.
They attacked Russia.
And we knew that the Germans were in town the night before, with the aid of the Ukrainians.
And, and we knew that the war broke out.
The Russians ran out with their undergarments.
They went to the camps directly.
The didn't even say goodbye to their wives and children.
And ran-- everybody was running and running in all directions.
Some people packed up.
They had a mode of communication, of, of traveling.
They take to their families and ran.
Others, young-- youth, youth, also, if they had bicycles, or whatever, they, they started running towards east.
They didn't want to remain under the Germans.
We couldn't run any place.
We were-- we didn't have a mode of, of traveling.
We didn't have a wagon.
But it happened so fast.
They were there.
Right then and there, the first day they were in town.
And the Russians started shooting back.
Then the-- apparently, the Germans-- whoever ran away from town, they were caught with the Germans the-- about 50 miles up.
Because the Germans came from both sides-- from the north and the south-- from Brest-Litovsk in the north and Volodymyr Volynsky in the south.
And they closed in like that and took over giant areas of Eastern Poland and the Ukraine.
And everybody was caught under the Germans.
So coming back, a lot of these people were killed on the roads by the Ukrainians.
And were-- the Ukrainians were, were robbing them and shooting them.
So they-- so those who didn't run were luckier at that point.
When we came in, the, the Germans--
We were still intact.
But the, the-- this was Sunday.
On Wednesday, the third day, the, the, the town went up in flames in several corners-- four or five different places.
The whole town was on fire.
There was no fire department.
There was no nothing.
And it just burned.
It burned down, the-- gutted the entire center of the town, which was the Jewish center.
The Jews lived in the center of the town.
And, and Ukrainians and Poles lived in the periphery.
And about 14, 15 streets burned down completely.
If I see?
I, I was there.
I was-- burning together.
The fire came--
The fire-- I remember the fire.
Everything was on fire till the fire came to my house, to our house.
And my father, he was always entrepreneuring.
And he grabbed buckets of water.
He ran on the ladder, and he put water on our house and stopped the fire from continuing, from marching on.
He figured, no matter what happens, we will have to have a place where to stay.
And he stopped the fire.
And the other houses burned down slowly.
And we kept watching the fire.
Then one German came up with a tricycle.
And he came over, du Jude, you Jew, if this house-- if this house-- [SPEAKING GERMAN].
This house will be burned.
You'll be shot immediately.

And we, we intended to, to save the house because now, there's several families of my father's immediate-- his parents, his-- my grandfather and grandmother and their children, and others-- about 20-some-odd people, they had no houses anymore.
So they all came to us.
And some people went in other places.
They drove them out of town, from the other side of the town.
And here, they, they were in a big ravine.
And everything around was burning.
Everything was on fire.
And I, I was catching those buckets and throwing it on the-- putting out the fire on the roof.
And we saved our house.
The date?
Yes.
It was the 25th, I believe-- the 25th of June, 1941.
I think it was a Tuesday or Wednesday.
And the whole town was, was guarded.
Then, they let us go back to the houses the following day.
And the fire was still burning all over and just chimneys remained.
All the houses were wood.
There were no brick houses.
So just chimneys, like dead chimneys, like a mortuary of chimneys-- wherever you looked, you could only see chimneys.
And at that point, we could see the synagogue from, from our house.
And before, we couldn't see it from the marketplace.
Now, we could see between the chimneys.
We saw the, the, the great synagogue in the middle.
So everybody came into our house, our family.
And we were 10, 15, 20 people in a room.
And that's how we, we settled.
We settled in, and the Germans were in town.
The next day, the Ukrainians took over the town.
They, they were the, the, the servants.
The, the girls-- the, the Ukrainian girls were standing with flowers, and so on.
And they'd offer themselves with everything.
And we Jews were, were caught in the middle.
And before we knew, we-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the, the-- in a few days, they put up signs that the Jews have to bring in anything of value.
They have to bring in to them.
And that it's gold, that's it's jewelry, whether money, or, or the foreign, foreign money, whatever, we had to bring in everything to the Germans.
I remember.
It was in Polish.
I remember it said in-- it was in Polish and Ukrainian and German.
It says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
The-- all the things of value must be brought into the Germans.
And then there were some other things.
These, these certain things, I remember.
It was engraved in my mind.
I would find some German papers, which-- the front papers.
And I remember it said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], Russia broke the pact with Russia-- with Germany.
And that's why they marched in.
Russia broke the pact, but they attacked.
So I remember that it was one title.
Every paper had one title, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Russia broke the pact, and that's how they started the war.
So these things, certain things, you remember.
We also had to start putting on an armband on the right side-- a white armband with the blue Star of David.
That was our marking.
And they start taking Jews to, to work.
Where did you-- where did you get the armbands?
We had to make it.
They didn't give you nothing.
They give you bullets only.
We had to take-- my mother had to take some material from, from some shirts, or whatever.
We had to start cutting all blue, blue signs-- blue stars on the right band.
And you would put it on, and you would attach it.
And that's how you would move around.
Then they also created the Judenrat.
And I don't have to explain what a Judenrat is.
It's a Jewish organization.
It would take care of Jewish affairs.
You had to supply workers to the government.
They needed workers at the railroad.
They needed workers for this or for that.
And the Jews had to come everyday.
And the Judenrat had to supply so many workers.
And they would divide where the workers would go.
We knew his name was Kalman Koppelsohn.
He was a-- he was a pharmacist, a very well-known person.
He was a, a leader before the war.
And he was a well-off man.
And he was appointed.
I think he didn't want to, but they-- he was appointed.
He was dragged into the job to be the Judenrat-- the Alteste of the Juden, the, the head of the Judenrat.
And he had a committee with him of people, visible people, who were-- who were live in communal life.
And they helped him in it.
Then they also had to make the Polish-- Jewish police and Jewish youth.
And the local youth didn't want to go.
So they had some of the refugees from other towns.
I remember two from Chelm.
They were in the-- in the Jewish police.
They said--
His name was Dunets, one I remember.
Another one was Freyman, from Poland also.
And they were in the-- in the Jewish-- it doesn't mean that they were bad people.
They were the ones who would lead the people.
And they had nothing, just a-- some kind of mini-uniform with a stick.
And that's all.
They would guide-- they would take the orders from the Ukrainians.
And they would take the orders from the Germans.
And they would lead the Jews accordingly to whatever they needed.
And when the Jews were led to work.
They would not go on sidewalk, but in the gutter.
So this was youth-- the Jewish police.
And then in five days, they gave an order that all the Jews have to be in the marketplace.
And they didn't know why.
Some people were hiding already.
They started looking for hiding places.
But whoever went to the marketplace, the [NON-ENGLISH], the, the, the, the, the men in charge, came and pulled out five prominent Jews.
He counted.
I think he counted every so-and-so and pulled them out.
I was not there.
But everybody--
--know.
Everybody knew about it because we were toying to go-- to go or not to.
I was a child.
I would do what my parents would, would do.
And they said maybe it's not good to go.
We didn't know what it meant.
But we heard shots.
And later on, they came back, and they said that they were-- they were-- five-- they killed five Jews.
Because they said that, that a telephone-- a German telephone line, a field telephone line was cut.
And, therefore, the Jews must have done it.
Therefore, they have to die.
They took them-- we had that little-- in the marketplace, we had a hill.
It's called [NON-ENGLISH].
It was a, a memorial place, a hill, where they-- for the King Jagiello, Polish King Jagiello from the 14th or 15th century.
And they took them on that place, and they shot them, all five.
Germans stood there.
And they just killed them.
And they took immediately to bury them.
They said this will serve a sign to you what we mean business.
It was the first sign we didn't really--
[SIGHS] I remember-- now, it escapes my memory.
I, I remember the names.
I saw them a hundred times.
They're written out in the-- in the-- in, in the yizkor book.
I just can't remember names now.
From the community.
They came with wagons.
They took the bodies and took them to the-- to the cemetery.
We had two cemeteries.
One was very old that dated back, like, 400 or 500 years.
That one was full.
And they took them to the newest cemetery out of town.
And, and they-- that's where they buried them.
Yeah, I mean, what are you going to do with bodies?
And then, things settled in slowly.
We, we knew things were getting bad and worse every day.
They would give us 200 gram of bread.
And everybody had to wear the armband.
And in, in a couple days, they had the first what we called in our tongue, [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] comes from [NON-ENGLISH], to grab, catch-- to catch people.
They came around in Mercedes trucks.
The Germans and Ukrainians.
It would be one German, or two Germans, and 15, 20 Ukrainians, whatever the German would say, Achtung!
And the German-- the, the Ukrainian would say, yeah, yeah, yeah.
They didn't know any, any German.
So the only word they ever said you say to a dog, go, seek!
They dog goes, bow, wow, wow.
That's the Ukrainians did, just like dogs.
They just wait for the-- for the German to give the order.
And they would go and fulfill very gladly, willingly.
And they would in the-- go around in the streets and would catch people.
They caught 400 men.
And they took them-- and they disappeared.
And--
It was about a month after they came in.
It must have been the-- see, June 1942?
This must have been somewheres in July, mid-July of '42.
And they had the first [NON-ENGLISH] where they-- where they grabbed 400 Jews.
Then they said, we want a contribution.
We want you to pay off.
You got to bring 30 pieces of gold, your gold coins.
And you got to bring 30 liters of vodka.
You got to bring boots and other things-- looting.
They called it a contribution-- [NON-ENGLISH].
But it was an extortion.
They said, you bring that, and we'll send you back the men.
Later on, we found out that on the fresh graves that they dug, they had a table set up with the vodka and all the food and everything.
And they-- the Germans and Ukrainians together were celebrating and drinking.
On top of the grave.
On the--
It was on the-- on the new cemetery grounds, which was to the west of the town.
We called it the [NON-ENGLISH], the New Cemetery.
And-- but my friend, Ab Getman, he lives in Long Beach.
He said that he went in the middle of the night-- I think his, his father was among them.
His father and his brother, I think, they were caught in it.
He went, and he snuck out in the middle of the night.
And he went, and he start digging.
And he found an armband.
And he knew that they were killed.
And it was a fresh grave-- and other people from the other sides that they were all killed and slaughtered the very same day.
A month after this was a second [NON-ENGLISH].
And at that point, I was already a little bigger boy.
And school?
There was no school.
There's no, no school or nothing for children.
So I worked in my father's machine shop.
They put up a Ukrainian.
He was in charge.
He was the boss.
From a Russian commissar, they put up a Ukrainian, who's now in charge under the Germans.
And whatever the Germans and Ukrainians would bring, we would have to do the work, my, my father and his two brothers.
And one other was taken-- was in-- was in-- mobilized before the war.
So he was in-- we didn't know where he was.
He was in the Russian Army-- or Polish Army, I believe-- either Polish or Russian Army.
And he was a prisoner of war some place.
We found out later that the Germans killed him in the town of Chelm.
But my father had an older brother, Gershon, and a younger brother, Iossel.
His, his middle brother, Zelig, he was in another town.
And he was mobilized in the Russian Army.
And he was taken off the front to Siberia.
I remember at one point, I cut my finger here with those machines.
The motor started to cut right here.
And one time, it hit me over here.
And I still have a cut here, and I have a cut right here.
And they would-- the, the Ukrainians and Germans, whatever they would bring, we would do the work.
And there was no pay.
It was just-- you do work for nothing.
And that's what it was.
It was a-- and life just got worse and worse, The Jews were led to work.
We were not.
We, we had our own machine shop.
So we, at least, didn't have to go through the-- this humiliation of going through all this struggle, and so on.
They would give us 100 gram of bread per person.
It was more sawdust and sand than, than, than anything else.
And they would come around and grab things in the house, whatever they would-- they would decide.
What kind of resistance?
To where?
Why-- the, the, the town was split.
We didn't even know what was going the other side of town.
There was no telephone.
We didn't have telephone.
We didn't have no radio.
We had no communication.
We had no newspapers.
We could not leave town.
We couldn't even go from one street to the next.
And this was-- this was life.
[SIGHS].
